records.notes: Small tree with mitten-shaped, unlobed, and three-lobed leaves often on the same plant. This entry keeps Sassafras as one tree-level page and treats leaf seasoning separately from historical root-bark/root-beer use.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, especially around bark/root preparations, but this guide does not recommend medicinal use. Root bark and root tea should be treated as historical use only because of safrole concerns.
records.culinary_uses: Dried sassafras leaves can be used as filé-style seasoning. Root bark and sassafras root tea are not recommended in this guide because safrole-containing sassafras has federal regulatory and safety concerns.
records.edibility_detail: Leaf use is the safer culinary angle: dried leaves may be used as filé-style seasoning. Do not use this app as a recommendation for sassafras root bark, sassafras oil, or root tea.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Roots / Tubers, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Use leaf seasoning only: dried sassafras leaves may be used as filé-style seasoning. Root bark, sassafras oil, and root tea are historical uses and are not recommended here because of safrole concerns.

records.safety_note: Federal food rules distinguish safrole-free sassafras extract from safrole-containing sassafras bark/root preparations. Do not treat homemade root bark tea or sassafras oil as equivalent to safrole-free commercial flavoring.

records.notes: Evergreen conifer with single sharp needles on small woody pegs and hanging cones. Young spring tips and resin/gum are the useful parts in this broad spruce entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use includes needle/tip tea and pitch/resin preparations; this guide treats those as traditional-use context.
records.culinary_uses: Tender new spring tips can be used fresh or cooked for tea, syrup, jelly, seasoning, or bright evergreen flavor. Resin/gum has traditional chew/topical use but is not bulk food.
records.plant_lane_note: Young spring tips belong in Leaves/Greens and Tea; resin/gum keeps it in Trees/Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Young soft spring tips are the useful edible/tea part; resin/gum is more traditional than routine food. Confirm true spruce before using.
records.edibility_detail: Use positively identified spruce. Young tips are the practical food/tea part; mature needles and resin are much more resinous.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with velvet twigs, alternate compound leaves, and dense upright red fruit clusters. Avoid white-berried poison sumac.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional astringent uses exist, though this is mostly a drink ingredient in the old app material.
records.culinary_uses: Fruit clusters are soaked in cool water to make tart drinks and can also flavor syrups and jellies.
records.edibility_detail: Edible fruit clusters, usually used as a souring ingredient rather than eaten whole.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible fruit clusters, usually used as a souring ingredient rather than eaten whole.

records.look_alike_notes: Staghorn sumac has upright red fuzzy fruit clusters and fuzzy twigs; poison sumac has whitish berries and grows in wetter swampy habitats. Do not use white-berried sumac.

records.notes: Woodland carrot-family plant with ferny leaves and sweet licorice-like scent.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic herb.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves, seeds, and roots can be used as sweet aromatic seasoning or tea ingredient.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but carrot-family caution applies: identification should be solid.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Roots / Tubers, Nuts / Seeds, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, root / tuber / bulb, seed / nut / cone. Edible, but carrot-family caution applies: identification should be solid.

records.look_alike_notes: Sweet cicely is a woodland Apiaceae; licorice-like aroma helps, but smell is not enough by itself. Separate it from deadly hemlocks with stem surface, blotching, habitat, leaf shape, and full plant confirmation. Also separate from wild parsnip, which has yellow-green umbels and sap that can cause sunlight-triggered burns.
records.risk_reason: Sweet cicely has edible/aromatic uses, but carrot-family look-alike risk is serious because white umbels and divided leaves can overlap with deadly species.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Ferny leaves, white umbels, and root/leaf use can tempt shortcuts in a plant family that includes deadly hemlocks.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat sweet cicely unless the plant is positively identified and the hemlock stop-sign checks are negative.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly hemlock confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Sweet cicely requires Apiaceae stop-sign checks. Do not rely on white umbels, ferny leaves, or scent alone.
required_checks: Stem hair vs. smoothness check required.
Purple/red blotch check required.
Habitat moisture check required.
Root/taste checks are not substitutes for full positive identification.
Confirm sweet-cicely woodland form and sweet/anise-like odor as supporting traits only.
Reject smooth purple-spotted stems and wetland root situations.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: If the plant has poison-hemlock or water-hemlock stop signs, do not eat it as sweet cicely.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — nervous-system poisoning and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly look-alike: smooth purple-spotted stems and hairless ferny leaves are poison-hemlock warning traits.
target_traits: Woodland sweet-cicely form with sweet/anise-like aroma as a supporting clue.
Identification uses the whole plant, not only divided leaves or white umbels.
lookalike_traits: Smooth, hairless, hollow stem instead of a bristly wild-carrot stem.
Purple or reddish blotches/spots on the stem or leaf stalks.
Glossy ferny divided leaves and white umbrella-like flower clusters.
Mousey, musty, or rank odor when crushed.
Deadly poisonous; can affect the nervous and respiratory systems.
required_checks: Check stem surface and purple/red blotches.
Check overall habitat and plant structure.
Do not rely on white umbels alone.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If a carrot-family plant has a smooth purple-spotted stem, do not eat it.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — seizures and rapid severe poisoning risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly wetland look-alike: unknown wetland Apiaceae roots are a hard no.
target_traits: Woodland sweet-cicely habit rather than marsh/ditch/shoreline habitat.
No thick or chambered water-hemlock root crown.
Full plant confirmation before any edible/aromatic use.
lookalike_traits: Wetland, marsh, ditch, shore, or streamside habitat raises the danger level immediately.
White umbels and compound leaves with toothed leaflets can overlap with edible carrot-family plants.
Thick root crown or chambered root structure is an extreme danger sign; roots are especially hazardous.
Do not dig, taste, chew, or smell-test unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Deadly poisonous; rapid severe poisoning and seizures are possible.
required_checks: Wetland habitat changes the ID problem immediately.
Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Reject thick/chambered root crowns.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.

records.notes: Wetland iris-like plant with aromatic rhizome and a side-emerging spadix. This is a caution species and not casual beginner food.
records.culinary_uses: Rhizome has traditional candying, tea, and flavoring use in very small quantities by people who know the plant well.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional digestive and aromatic medicinal plant.

records.culinary_uses: Aromatic leaves are mainly used for tea, seasoning, or infusion rather than as a vegetable.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic plant used in teas and simple preparations.

records.notes: Soft-leaved bramble with very large maple-like leaves and broad flattened red fruits.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry entry.
records.culinary_uses: Soft red berries are sweet and excellent but fragile, often best eaten quickly or turned into jam.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but delicate and not very shelf-stable.

records.notes: Spring woodland mustard-family plant with paired or whorled leaves depending on species and white to pale pink flowers. Also called crinkleroot or pepper root.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and roots/rhizomes have peppery mustard-family food use, raw or cooked, in small quantities.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves and roots/rhizomes are the useful parts; flowers help with spring ID.
records.plant_card_note: Peppery spring woodland mustard relative; leaves and roots/rhizomes are the useful parts in small amounts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible as a peppery spring green/root in small amounts. Confirm Cardamine/toothwort traits before using.

records.culinary_uses: Good fresh berry and preserve berry, often on smaller low plants.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.

records.notes: Low woodland and lawn flowers with heart-shaped leaves and purple, blue, yellow, or white blooms depending on species.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing herb.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and flowers are used in salads, teas, candies, and mild cooked greens.
records.edibility_detail: Widely edible leaves and flowers, especially young leaves.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Tea / Infusions.

records.notes: Bramble with thorny canes and aggregate fruits that keep the core when picked, unlike raspberries.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh or cooked berry used in jam, syrup, cobbler, baking, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Harvest dark glossy berries at full ripeness.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.

records.notes: Biennial Apiaceae with first-year rosettes, feathery leaves, hairy stems, white umbrella flower clusters, and a carrot-scented taproot. Serious hemlock confusion risk: smooth purple-spotted stems or wetland carrot-family roots are stop signs.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use exists, but this guide treats it as a caution-heavy root entry only.
records.culinary_uses: First-year taproots are the food-side part, but only with expert-level identification; this is not a beginner nibble.
records.plant_lane_note: Root is the useful part; flowers/leaves are ID clues, not extra food lanes.
records.plant_card_note: Expert-only root entry. First-year roots are the possible use, but deadly hemlock confusion and wild-parsnip sap-burn risk make this a hard caution record, not a beginner plant.
records.edibility_detail: Expert-only Apiaceae root entry. Do not treat Wild carrot / Queen Anne’s lace as a beginner root food. Use only a positively identified first-year Daucus carota root from a dry/open site after poison hemlock, water hemlock, and wild parsnip have been ruled out. White umbels, lacy leaves, or carrot smell are not enough.

records.look_alike_notes: Wild carrot is a dangerous Apiaceae comparison plant, not a casual “free carrot.” Do not rely on white umbels, lacy leaves, or carrot smell. Separate it from poison hemlock, water hemlock, and wild parsnip using stem hair/surface, purple blotching, habitat, root structure, flower color, bracts, and sap-burn risk.
records.risk_reason: Apiaceae confusion can be deadly: wild carrot can be mistaken for poison hemlock or water hemlock, and it can be confused with wild parsnip, whose sap can burn sun-exposed skin.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: White/yellow umbels and divided leaves are shared by several carrot-family plants; smooth purple-spotted stems, wetland roots, and yellow wild-parsnip umbels with burn-causing sap are major danger traits.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat wild carrot unless the plant is positively identified as Daucus carota and poison hemlock, water hemlock, and wild parsnip have been ruled out. Smooth purple-spotted stems, wetland habitat, thick/chambered roots, yellow wild-parsnip umbels, or sap-burn risk mean stop.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly hemlock confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Wild carrot has dangerous Apiaceae look-alikes. White umbels and lacy leaves are not enough; stem hair, purple blotches, habitat, bracts, odor, yellow wild-parsnip flowers, sap-burn risk, and root context all matter.
required_checks: Stem hair vs. smoothness check required.
Purple/red blotch check required.
Habitat moisture check required.
Root/taste checks are not substitutes for full positive identification.
Queen Anne’s lace bracts and hairy/bristly stem should be present for wild carrot.
Reject wetland carrot-family plants for root use.
Yellow/greenish umbels and coarse parsnip-like leaves point away from Wild carrot and toward wild parsnip/contact-burn caution.
Avoid bare-handed handling of unknown Apiaceae; wild parsnip sap can burn skin after sunlight exposure.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Smooth purple-spotted stems, unknown wetland Apiaceae roots, or yellow wild-parsnip umbels/contact-burn signs mean do not eat as Wild carrot.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — nervous-system poisoning and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly look-alike: smooth purple-spotted stems, hairless foliage, and a mousey/musty odor are stop signs.
target_traits: Hairy or bristly stem; not smooth and hairless.
Lacy, finely divided leaves and a carrot smell from a crushed confirmed first-year root.
White Queen Anne’s lace umbel with visible bracts below it; a dark central flower may be present but is not required.
Usually dry/open disturbed habitat such as fields, roadsides, or dry open ground.
Food use is limited to a positively identified first-year root in a safe context.
lookalike_traits: Smooth, hairless, hollow stem instead of a bristly wild-carrot stem.
Purple or reddish blotches/spots on the stem or leaf stalks.
Glossy ferny divided leaves and white umbrella-like flower clusters.
Mousey, musty, or rank odor when crushed.
Deadly poisonous; can affect the nervous and respiratory systems.
required_checks: Stem must be hairy/bristly for wild carrot.
Reject smooth hairless stems with purple/red blotches.
Do not rely on white umbels alone.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If a carrot-family plant has a smooth purple-spotted stem, do not eat it.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — rapid nervous-system poisoning, seizures, and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly wetland look-alike: wet habitat plus carrot-family white umbels or thick roots is a hard stop.
target_traits: Hairy or bristly stem; not smooth and hairless.
Lacy, finely divided leaves and a carrot smell from a crushed confirmed first-year root.
White Queen Anne’s lace umbel with visible bracts below it; a dark central flower may be present but is not required.
Usually dry/open disturbed habitat such as fields, roadsides, or dry open ground.
Food use is limited to a positively identified first-year root in a safe context.
lookalike_traits: Wetland, marsh, ditch, shore, or streamside habitat raises the danger level immediately.
White umbels and compound leaves with toothed leaflets can overlap with edible carrot-family plants.
Thick root crown or chambered root structure is an extreme danger sign; roots are especially hazardous.
Do not dig, taste, chew, or smell-test unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Deadly poisonous; rapid severe poisoning and seizures are possible.
required_checks: Dry/open disturbed habitat supports wild carrot; wetland habitat does not.
Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Treat chambered or thick root crowns as deadly danger traits.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Dangerous contact-burn plant; edible root only with serious caution.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Dangerous look-alike / caution plant: yellow-green umbels and sap that can cause severe sunlight-triggered burns. The root is edible only with certain ID and protective handling.
target_traits: Wild carrot has white Queen Anne’s lace umbels, fine lacy leaves, and a hairy/bristly stem.
Food use is limited to a positively identified first-year Wild carrot root in a safe dry/open context.
Wild carrot should not have yellow-green wild-parsnip flower clusters.
lookalike_traits: Yellow to yellow-green flat-topped compound umbels rather than white Queen Anne’s lace flowers.
Coarse compound leaves with 5-15 toothed leaflets, not the same fine lacy Wild carrot leaf texture.
Stout hollow grooved stem; plant often appears along sunny roadsides, fields, and disturbed open ground.
Sap from leaves, stems, flowers, and fruit can cause severe photodermatitis after sunlight exposure.
First-year taproot is edible with serious caution, but this does not make the plant casual or beginner-safe.
required_checks: Check flower color: yellow/greenish umbels are not Wild carrot.
Avoid sap contact; wear gloves and long sleeves around suspected wild parsnip.
Do not dig or taste unknown Apiaceae roots.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Yellow wild-parsnip umbels or sap exposure risk means do not treat the plant as Wild carrot.

records.notes: Low woodland groundcover with paired heart-shaped leaves and hidden brown-purple flowers near the ground. Historical rhizome use exists, but safety concerns make this a questionable food entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Historical medicinal use exists, but internal use needs caution and is not recommended by this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Historical rhizome flavoring only; not treated as a practical edible plant in this guide until safety is reviewed.
records.plant_lane_note: Kept temporarily as a root entry but flagged because food-side use is questionable.
records.plant_card_note: Historical rhizome/flavoring plant, but modern safety concerns make it a candidate to move out of edible Plants.
records.edibility_detail: Flagged for safety review. This may belong outside the edible Plants section because of modern safety concerns around wild ginger/internal use.

records.notes: Broad mustard-family greens bucket. Look for peppery young leaves, four-petaled yellow or white flowers, and slender seed pods; avoid sprayed roadsides, farm edges, or polluted sites.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves are the main use and are best before the plant gets tough and strongly bitter. Flower buds, flowers, and mature seeds can also be used as peppery seasoning.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Nuts / Seeds.
records.plant_card_note: Young peppery leaves are the main use; buds, flowers, and mature seeds can season food. Avoid sprayed or polluted disturbed ground.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with correct mustard-family identification. Older plants can be tough or bitter; avoid contaminated or sprayed sites.
look_alikes: Young non-mustard weeds in disturbed soil

records.notes: Shrubby to small-tree plums with spring white blossom and yellow to red fruits depending on species and strain.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit-tree entry.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are eaten fresh or made into jelly, butter, preserves, and desserts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit; tart skins and clingstone pits are common.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit; tart skins and clingstone pits are common.

records.notes: Shrubby rose with prickly stems, compound leaves, pink flowers, and red to orange hips in late season.
records.culinary_uses: Fruits can be used for tea, syrup, jelly, and puree once the irritating inner hairs and seeds are handled carefully.
records.medicinal_uses: Vitamin-C-rich traditional winter tea and tonic fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Flowers, Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: flower, fruit / berry. Fruits can be used for tea, syrup, jelly, and puree once the irritating inner hairs and seeds are handled carefully.

records.notes: Low perennial with trifoliate toothed leaves, white five-petaled flowers, runners, and small red strawberries.
records.medicinal_uses: Leaf tea has traditional use, but this guide treats it mainly as a fruit and tea plant.
records.culinary_uses: Small ripe berries are eaten fresh or used like cultivated strawberries; leaves can be used for mild tea.
records.plant_lane_note: Fruit is the main part; flowers help ID the plant, and leaves are a secondary tea use.
records.plant_card_note: Small red fruits are the main prize; look for white flowers, trifoliate toothed leaves, and runners.
records.edibility_detail: Ripe fruit is edible. Leaves are a secondary tea use; avoid confusing with bland mock strawberry if the fruit/flower traits do not match.

records.notes: Tree or shrub of wet ground and waterways with narrow leaves on many species. This is a medicinal-first entry in the new app.

records.culinary_uses: Not a primary food plant; practical use centers more on medicinal bark and occasional emergency sap/cambium traditions than on casual eating.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic bark medicine associated with pain, fever, and inflammation traditions.

records.edibility_detail: Not a routine food entry. Bark use belongs in the medicine lane and carries medication-style cautions.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: bark / cambium, leaf / tea. Not a routine food entry. Bark use belongs in the medicine lane and carries medication-style cautions.

records.notes: Low evergreen woodland plant with glossy leaves, white urn flowers, and red berry-like fruits.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea and aromatic medicinal plant.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and berries are used in tea, candying, and flavoring rather than as bulk food.

records.edibility_detail: Edible in modest use as tea, flavoring, or small fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry, leaf / tea. Edible in modest use as tea, flavoring, or small fruit.

records.notes: Low shamrock-like Oxalis with trifoliate leaves and small delicate flowers. This consolidated record also absorbs the old yellow wood-sorrel part record.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a flavoring green here.
records.culinary_uses: Tangy leaves and flowers are used fresh in small amounts as a sour accent, not as a bulk green.
records.edibility_detail: Edible in modest amounts; tart oxalic-acid flavor is the point, not heavy eating.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers.
records.plant_card_note: Tangy leaves and flowers are useful as a sour accent in small amounts; this is not a bulk green.

records.notes: Aromatic perennial with finely divided feathery leaves and flat-topped clusters of small white to pale flowers.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves are a bitter aromatic herb in small amounts; flowers and leaves are more commonly used for tea.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea and wound/herbal plant; this guide treats it as tea/traditional-use context, not medical advice.

images_structured.title: Yarrow habit
images_structured.author: Pablo Alberto Salguero Quiles
images_structured.credit: Pablo Alberto Salguero Quiles, CC BY-SA 3.0/GFDL, via Wikimedia Commons

records.plant_lane_note: Flowers/leaves are mainly tea/herb parts; leaf use as a green is small-quantity only.
records.plant_card_note: Aromatic bitter leaves and flowers are mostly a tea/herb use, not a bulk food. Keep safety context visible.
records.edibility_detail: Use as a small-quantity bitter herb or tea plant. Avoid heavy use and review safety if pregnant, on medication, or sensitive to Asteraceae plants.

records.notes: Aquatic plant with floating or emergent leaves and yellow cup-like flowers. This is a broad pond-lily food-use record focused on underground parts and seeds.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use exists, but this guide treats it mainly as a difficult aquatic food plant.
records.culinary_uses: Traditional food use centers on cooked rhizomes/roots and seeds that can be popped or ground into meal.
records.plant_lane_note: Rhizomes/roots and seeds are the useful parts; flowers are included mostly as ID context.
records.plant_card_note: Aquatic, processing-heavy plant: cooked rhizomes/roots and seeds are the useful parts. Water quality and legal harvest matter.
records.edibility_detail: Processing-heavy aquatic food. Harvest legality, water quality, and correct ID matter before any use.

images_structured.title: Yellow pond lily in bloom
images_structured.author: Jomegat
images_structured.credit: Jomegat
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.

records.commonness: Locally common invasive in suitable disturbed/wet-edge habitats
records.food_quality: Edible young shoots with invasive-spread warning
records.culinary_uses: Young spring shoots can be cooked like a sour rhubarb-like vegetable when harvested from clean, unsprayed sites. Mature stems become tough and are not a food target.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional-use/invasive-plant discussions exist, but this guide keeps medical claims conservative.
records.other_uses: Invasive-plant awareness: learn it so you do not spread it by moving rhizomes, stem fragments, or contaminated soil.
records.edibility_detail: Edible young shoots only. Do not transport fragments, rhizomes, or soil; do not compost pieces casually. Avoid treated/sprayed colonies.
records.notes: Hollow bamboo-like stems with raised nodes, zigzag stems, reddish new growth, broad leaves, dense colonies, and creamy-white late-season flower sprays. The ecological warning is as important as the food use.
records.general_notes: Hollow bamboo-like stems with raised nodes, zigzag stems, reddish new growth, broad leaves, dense colonies, and creamy-white late-season flower sprays. The ecological warning is as important as the food use.
records.plant_lane_note: Young shoots can be food; the hard rule is do not spread fragments or soil.
records.plant_card_note: Young shoots can be food; the hard rule is do not spread fragments or soil.

images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution not required by the listed public-domain/CC0 status, but courtesy credit is retained.

records.commonness: Common in damp/open and disturbed habitats
records.food_quality: Caution / traditional-use plant, not a food target
records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food plant in this guide.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal-use plant, but the guide should keep claims conservative and caution-heavy.
records.other_uses: Silica-rich stems have a scouring/utility history.
records.edibility_detail: Do not present as casual food or casual tea. Species-level ID matters, and horsetails have documented toxicity concerns in livestock and caution contexts.
records.notes: Ancient-looking plant with jointed hollow stems. Early fertile cone shoots appear in spring; later green sterile stems have whorled bottle-brush branches.
records.general_notes: Ancient-looking plant with jointed hollow stems. Early fertile cone shoots appear in spring; later green sterile stems have whorled bottle-brush branches.
records.plant_lane_note: Caution-heavy ID record; not a food recommendation.
records.plant_card_note: Caution-heavy ID record; not a food recommendation.
usable_parts: Traditional-use stems only; not a food target
